subject: Chemistry
title: Hydrogen Bonds: Water's Secret
description: A quick, practical guide to Hydrogen Bonds: Water's Secret.

hook.text: Water shouldn't be liquid at room temperature. It's too small a molecule. Hydrogen bonds are why it is.

buildup.text: Oxygen pulls electron density away from hydrogen, creating a partial negative charge on O and partial positive on H.

discovery.text: The positive H of one water molecule attracts the negative O of another. This weak bond forms and breaks constantly.

twist.text: Without hydrogen bonds, water would boil at -80°C—like hydrogen sulfide. Earth would have no liquid water, and no life.

climax.text: DNA's double helix is held together by hydrogen bonds between base pairs. They're strong enough to hold, weak enough to unzip.

punchline.text: A weak bond that makes water liquid and holds DNA together. Life's essential glue.

quiz.question: Why is water liquid at room temperature despite being a very small molecule?
options: Hydrogen bonds between water molecules raise the boiling point dramatically
Oxygen atoms are very heavy, keeping molecules close
Water molecules are magnetic and attract each other
